section.title: The Kings of Israel and Judah
section.sectionRationale: This chapter races through decades of instability in Israel, four kings in rapid succession, three of them murdered by their own successors, while Judah enjoys the far steadier, though still incomplete, reigns of Azariah and Jotham. Assyria's Tiglath-pileser first appears, foreshadowing the coming Assyrian domination that will soon end the northern kingdom altogether.
section.thesis: As Israel's throne becomes a revolving door of assassination while Judah's kings, though flawed, provide relative stability, the LORD's warnings through the prophets Amos and Hosea about Israel's coming collapse begin visibly bearing out in the nation's own escalating political chaos.
section.frameNote: Four beats: Azariah's long but leprous reign in Judah, verses 1 to 7; Zechariah and Shallum's brief, violent reigns in Israel, verses 8 to 16; Menahem and Pekahiah's reigns under rising Assyrian pressure, verses 17 to 26; and Pekah's reign and Jotham's steadier rule in Judah, verses 27 to 38.

units.label: Azariah's long reign in Judah
units.note: the LORD afflicted the king with leprosy

verses.text: In the twenty-seventh year of Jeroboam's reign over Israel, Azariah son of Amaziah became king of Judah.
He was sixteen years old when he became king, and he reigned in Jerusalem fifty-two years. His mother's name was Jecoliah; she was from Jerusalem.
And he did what was right in the eyes of the LORD, just as his father Amaziah had done.
Nevertheless, the high places were not taken away; the people continued sacrificing and burning incense there.

And the LORD afflicted the king with {{tsaraath|leprosy}} until the day he died, so that he lived in a separate house while his son Jotham had charge of the palace and governed the people of the land.
As for the rest of the acts of Azariah, along with all his accomplishments, are they not written in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Judah?
And Azariah rested with his fathers and was buried near them in the City of David. And his son Jotham reigned in his place.

units.label: Zechariah and Shallum's violent reigns
units.note: struck him down and killed him

verses.text: In the thirty-eighth year of Azariah's reign over Judah, Zechariah son of Jeroboam became king of Israel, and he reigned in Samaria six months.
And he did evil in the sight of the LORD, as his fathers had done. He did not turn away from the sins that Jeroboam son of Nebat had caused Israel to commit.
Then Shallum son of Jabesh conspired against Zechariah, struck him down and killed him in front of the people, and reigned in his place.
As for the rest of the acts of Zechariah, they are indeed written in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Israel.
So the word of the LORD spoken to Jehu was fulfilled: "Four generations of your sons will sit on the throne of Israel."
In the thirty-ninth year of Uzziah's reign over Judah, Shallum son of Jabesh became king, and he reigned in Samaria one full month.
Then Menahem son of Gadi went up from Tirzah to Samaria, struck down and killed Shallum son of Jabesh, and reigned in his place.
As for the rest of the acts of Shallum, along with the {{qesher|conspiracy}} he led, they are indeed written in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Israel.
At that time Menahem, starting from Tirzah, attacked Tiphsah and everyone in its vicinity, because they would not open their gates. So he attacked Tiphsah and ripped open all the pregnant women.

units.label: Menahem and Pekahiah under Assyrian pressure
units.note: Pul king of Assyria invaded the land

verses.text: In the thirty-ninth year of Azariah's reign over Judah, Menahem son of Gadi became king of Israel, and he reigned in Samaria ten years.
And he did evil in the sight of the LORD, and throughout his reign he did not turn away from the sins that Jeroboam son of Nebat had caused Israel to commit.
Then Pul king of Assyria invaded the land, and Menahem gave Pul a thousand talents of silver in order to gain his support and strengthen his own grip on the kingdom.

Menahem exacted this {{mas|tax}} from each of the wealthy men of Israel, fifty shekels of silver from each man, to give to the king of Assyria. So the king of Assyria withdrew and did not remain in the land.
As for the rest of the acts of Menahem, along with all his accomplishments, are they not written in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Israel?
And Menahem rested with his fathers, and his son Pekahiah reigned in his place.
In the fiftieth year of Azariah's reign over Judah, Pekahiah son of Menahem became king of Israel and reigned in Samaria two years.
And he did evil in the sight of the LORD and did not turn away from the sins that Jeroboam son of Nebat had caused Israel to commit.

Then his {{gibbor|officer}}, Pekah son of Remaliah, conspired against him along with Argob, Arieh, and fifty men of Gilead. And at the citadel of the king's palace in Samaria, Pekah struck down and killed Pekahiah and reigned in his place.
As for the rest of the acts of Pekahiah, along with all his accomplishments, they are indeed written in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Israel.

units.label: Pekah's reign and Jotham's rule in Judah

verses.text: In the fifty-second year of Azariah's reign over Judah, Pekah son of Remaliah became king of Israel, and he reigned in Samaria twenty years.
And he did evil in the sight of the LORD and did not turn away from the sins that Jeroboam son of Nebat had caused Israel to commit.

In the days of Pekah king of Israel, Tiglath-pileser king of Assyria came and captured Ijon, Abel-beth-maacah, Janoah, Kedesh, Hazor, Gilead, and Galilee, including all the land of Naphtali, and he took the people as captives to Assyria.
Then Hoshea son of Elah led a conspiracy against Pekah son of Remaliah. In the twentieth year of Jotham son of Uzziah, Hoshea attacked Pekah, killed him, and reigned in his place.
As for the rest of the acts of Pekah, along with all his accomplishments, they are indeed written in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Israel.
In the second year of the reign of Pekah son of Remaliah over Israel, Jotham son of Uzziah became king of Judah.
He was twenty-five years old when he became king, and he reigned in Jerusalem sixteen years. His mother's name was Jerusha daughter of Zadok.
And he did what was right in the eyes of the LORD, just as his father Uzziah had done.
Nevertheless, the high places were not taken away; the people continued sacrificing and burning incense there. Jotham rebuilt the Upper Gate of the house of the LORD.
As for the rest of the acts of Jotham, along with his accomplishments, are they not written in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Judah?
(In those days the LORD began to send Rezin king of Aram and Pekah son of Remaliah against Judah.)
And Jotham rested with his fathers and was buried with them in the City of David his father. And his son Ahaz reigned in his place.

tsaraath.gloss: A skin disease rendered leprosy, treated as ritually defiling in Leviticus 13 to 14. That the LORD personally afflicts Azariah with tsaraath, forcing him into isolation for the rest of his reign, marks a specific, ongoing divine discipline even upon a generally righteous king whose reform remained incomplete, the high places never removed.

mas.gloss: Forced tax, tribute, or corvee labor. Menahem's mas, extracted from Israel's wealthy to buy off Assyrian withdrawal, previews the crushing tribute burden that will only intensify as Assyria's shadow lengthens over Israel in the chapters to come.

qesher.gloss: Conspiracy or treasonous plot. This chapter records qesher after qesher, Shallum against Zechariah, Menahem's rise, Pekah against Pekahiah, Hoshea against Pekah, a relentless pattern of political violence that visibly enacts the instability Hosea would soon describe as Israel devouring its own leaders, Hosea 7:7.

gibbor.gloss: A mighty man, warrior, or high military officer. That Pekah, Pekahiah's own gibbor, a trusted officer, is the one who assassinates him shows that Israel's military strength had turned inward, its commanders consuming the nation's own leadership rather than defending it from real external threats like Assyria.

basic.context: This chapter compresses decades of contrasting national experience. In Judah, Azariah, also called Uzziah, reigns fifty-two years generally faithfully, though struck with leprosy for an unspecified sin, and is followed by his similarly faithful but incomplete son Jotham. In Israel, meanwhile, four kings pass through the throne in rapid, often bloody succession, Zechariah assassinated by Shallum, Shallum himself assassinated within a month by Menahem, Menahem's son Pekahiah assassinated by his own officer Pekah, and Pekah eventually assassinated by Hoshea. Assyria's king first appears during Menahem's reign, extracting heavy tribute, and reappears under Pekah, seizing large portions of Israelite territory, an ominous foreshadowing of the nation's approaching end.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative rapidly summarizing several reigns through the author's consistent theological lens; it is not merely a chronicle of political trivia but a deliberate demonstration of covenant consequences unfolding in real history. What endures is the pattern that persistent, unaddressed national sin eventually produces visible instability and vulnerability, exactly as the covenant warnings and contemporary prophets like Amos and Hosea had been declaring.
basic.originalLanguages: Azariah's tsaraath, leprosy, marks specific divine discipline even on a relatively faithful king. Menahem's mas, forced tribute, to Assyria previews the crushing burden soon to come. And repeated qesher, conspiracy, tears through Israel's throne again and again, exposing a kingdom whose own gibborim, mighty men, had turned their strength inward against their own rulers rather than outward in faithful defense of the nation.
basic.typology: Israel's revolving door of violent, short-lived kings, contrasted with Judah's relatively more stable line under David's covenant promise, previews the ultimate contrast between all merely human, unstable kingship and the eternal, unshakeable kingdom secured under Christ, the true and everlasting King.
basic.christ: The chaos of Israel's kingship, marked by repeated assassination and no lasting stability whatsoever, throws into sharp relief the promise given specifically to David's line, and ultimately fulfilled in Christ, whose throne, unlike every human dynasty recorded in this chapter, will never be seized, ended, or replaced, Luke 1:32-33, Hebrews 1:8.
basic.god: God's covenant warnings are not abstract threats but work themselves out through real historical events, political chaos, foreign tribute, and territorial loss, exactly as persistent unfaithfulness accumulates consequences. Yet God remains patient even amid this decline, preserving Judah's Davidic line through imperfect but relatively faithful kings like Azariah and Jotham, while Israel's own instability increasingly exposes it to the coming Assyrian threat.
basic.crossReferences: Hosea 7:3-7 vividly describes Israel devouring its own kings through repeated conspiracy, precisely the pattern this chapter records in real historical detail. Amos 7:9-11 prophesies against the house of Jeroboam during this very era, and Isaiah 7:1 references the very alliance of Rezin and Pekah against Judah mentioned in verse 37. Luke 1:32-33 promises Christ an everlasting throne, the ultimate contrast to Israel's revolving door of assassinated kings.
basic.oneStory: As Israel's throne becomes a bloody revolving door and Assyria's shadow begins to fall over the land, while Judah's more stable but still incomplete kings continue under God's covenant patience, this chapter shows covenant unfaithfulness working itself out in real historical consequences, previewing the eventual collapse of the northern kingdom and pointing toward the need for a King whose throne can never be shaken, fulfilled only in Jesus Christ.

context.literary: The chapter's rapid, formulaic structure, repeating the same pattern of accession, evaluation, and death for king after king in quick succession, itself conveys the instability it describes, especially in Israel, where the narrative rhythm accelerates noticeably compared to the more measured accounts given for Judah's kings.
context.historical: Pul and Tiglath-pileser are the same Assyrian king, known by two names in different sources, whose westward campaigns during this period are independently attested in Assyrian records listing tribute from Menahem of Israel by name. The territories Tiglath-pileser captures under Pekah, Galilee, Gilead, and Naphtali, correspond to the northern and eastern regions of Israel, consistent with the historical pattern of Assyrian conquest proceeding from the periphery inward before Samaria's eventual fall.
context.canonical: This chapter's kings form the direct historical and prophetic backdrop for the ministries of Amos and Hosea, whose oracles against Israel's corrupt leadership, idolatry, and coming judgment address precisely this era, while Isaiah's ministry to Judah begins during Uzziah's reign, Isaiah 6:1, and continues through the alliance crisis of Rezin and Pekah against Judah described here in verse 37 and expanded in Isaiah 7.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To trace, in compressed but pointed form, how decades of persistent covenant unfaithfulness in Israel produced escalating political chaos and vulnerability to Assyria, while Judah's more stable, though still incomplete, kings preserved a measure of order under God's ongoing covenant patience.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter narrates specific historical successions and their political consequences; it does not prescribe political assassination as a means of achieving power, nor does it suggest that national stability alone is proof of righteousness, since even Judah's relatively steadier kings still failed to remove the high places. What endures is the pattern of consequences that persistent unaddressed sin produces at a national level.

debates.question: For what specific sin was Azariah struck with leprosy, since the text here does not explain the cause directly?

views.label: Pride leading to unlawful priestly intrusion, per 2 Chronicles 26
views.case: 2 Chronicles 26:16-21 explains that Azariah, called Uzziah there, grew proud after his successes and unlawfully entered the temple to burn incense, a priestly function reserved for Aaron's descendants, and was struck with leprosy immediately as the priests confronted him, a fuller account that this briefer summary in Kings presupposes without repeating.

views.label: An unspecified divine judgment left deliberately unexplained in Kings
views.case: The author of Kings may deliberately leave the specific cause unstated here, consistent with this book's more compressed style for northern kingdom parallel material, expecting readers already familiar with the fuller Chronicles tradition or simply emphasizing the fact and result of divine discipline rather than its precise occasion.

debates.bearing: Reading this account alongside 2 Chronicles 26 fills out a pattern seen elsewhere in Kings, that pride following genuine success, as with Amaziah in the previous chapter, remains a recurring danger even for otherwise faithful kings, inviting real and lasting divine discipline.

study.originalLanguages: Azariah's affliction with tsaraath, leprosy, marks specific divine discipline that isolates him for the remainder of his reign. Menahem's mas, tribute exacted from Israel's wealthy to satisfy Assyria, previews the far heavier burden soon to fall on the whole nation. And relentless qesher, conspiracy, tears through Israel's throne four times in this chapter alone, exposing a kingdom whose gibborim, mighty men and commanders, had turned their strength against their own kings rather than toward faithful national defense.

typology.type: A revolving door of assassinated, short-reigned kings with no lasting stability
typology.fulfillment: The everlasting, unshakeable throne promised to David's greater Son, Jesus Christ, whose kingdom, unlike every human dynasty, will never end, Luke 1:32-33.

typology.type: A nation's own strength turned inward, consuming its own leadership rather than defending against outside threat
typology.fulfillment: The need for a true Shepherd-King who protects rather than preys upon His own people, John 10:11-12.

study.christ: The relentless chaos of Israel's kingship in this chapter, four kings in rapid succession, three of them murdered by the very men who succeeded them, throws into sharp relief the covenant promise given uniquely to David's line, a promise fulfilled at last in Christ, whose throne, unlike every fragile human dynasty catalogued here, can never be seized, ended, or replaced, Luke 1:32-33, Hebrews 1:8. Where Israel's own commanders devoured their own kings, Christ is the true Shepherd-King who lays down His own life for His sheep rather than preying upon them, John 10:11.
study.god: God's covenant warnings prove to be no empty threat but work themselves out through real historical events, political assassination, crushing foreign tribute, and territorial loss, as persistent national unfaithfulness accumulates real consequences. Yet God remains patient even in the midst of this unraveling, preserving Judah's Davidic line through imperfect yet relatively faithful kings, showing that His covenant commitment endures even while His justice against sin becomes increasingly visible in the surrounding chaos.

crossReferences.note: Explains the specific cause of Azariah's leprosy, his prideful, unlawful intrusion into priestly duties, filling out the brief mention in this chapter.
Describes Israel devouring its own kings through repeated conspiracy, a vivid prophetic commentary on precisely the instability recorded here.
References the alliance of Rezin of Aram and Pekah of Israel against Judah mentioned in verse 37, providing the backdrop for Isaiah's famous prophecy to Ahaz.
Promises Christ an everlasting throne, the ultimate and final contrast to Israel's chaotic and repeatedly overthrown kingship in this chapter.

study.oneStory: As Israel's throne becomes a violent revolving door and Assyria's shadow first falls ominously across the land, while Judah's comparatively steadier but still incomplete kings continue under God's patient covenant faithfulness, this chapter shows persistent national unfaithfulness working itself out in real historical consequences. It anticipates both the imminent collapse of the northern kingdom and the deeper need for a King whose throne can never be shaken by conspiracy or war, a need met only in Jesus Christ, the true and eternal Son of David.

quiz.q: What divine discipline fell upon Azariah despite his generally righteous reign, and how did it affect his rule?

options.text: The LORD afflicted him with leprosy for the rest of his life, forcing him to live separately while his son Jotham governed the people.
options.note: Verse 5 records this exact discipline and its practical governmental consequence.

options.text: He was completely removed from the throne and exiled from Judah.
options.note: He remained king in title while his son governed daily affairs; he was not exiled from the nation.

options.text: He experienced no discipline at all despite the high places remaining.
options.note: The text explicitly records the LORD afflicting him with leprosy specifically.

options.text: His entire family was struck with the same affliction.
options.note: The text specifies the affliction fell on Azariah himself, not his entire family.

quiz.q: How many kings of Israel are recorded in this chapter, and how did most of them come to power or lose it?

options.text: Four kings, Zechariah, Shallum, Menahem, Pekahiah, and Pekah, with most gaining or losing the throne through violent conspiracy and assassination.
options.note: The chapter records Zechariah assassinated by Shallum, Shallum by Menahem, Pekahiah by Pekah, and Pekah later by Hoshea, a repeated pattern of violence.

options.text: Only one king reigned throughout this entire period in Israel.
options.note: The chapter records multiple distinct kings in rapid succession, not a single long reign.

options.text: All the kings named succeeded peacefully through normal hereditary succession.
options.note: Most transitions described involved violent conspiracy and assassination, not peaceful hereditary succession.

options.text: No specific kings of Israel are named in this chapter.
options.note: Several specific kings are named and described in detail throughout the chapter.

quiz.q: What did Menahem do in response to the invasion of Pul, king of Assyria?

options.text: He paid Pul a massive tribute of a thousand talents of silver, extracted through taxing Israel's wealthy men, to secure Assyria's withdrawal and strengthen his own grip on power.
options.note: Verses 19 to 20 record this exact tribute payment and its stated purpose.

options.text: He defeated Pul's army in open battle.
options.note: Menahem paid tribute rather than achieving a military victory over the Assyrian king.

options.text: He refused to engage with Assyria in any way.
options.note: Menahem actively engaged by paying a specific, large tribute rather than avoiding Assyria entirely.

options.text: Assyria conquered Israel completely at this time.
options.note: The text states Assyria withdrew after receiving tribute rather than conquering Israel at this point.

quiz.q: What significant territorial losses occurred during Pekah's reign?

options.text: Tiglath-pileser of Assyria captured Ijon, Abel-beth-maacah, Janoah, Kedesh, Hazor, Gilead, and Galilee, including all of Naphtali, taking the people captive to Assyria.
options.note: Verse 29 lists these specific territorial losses and the resulting captivity.

options.text: Israel gained significant new territory from Assyria during this period.
options.note: The text describes substantial territorial loss to Assyria, not territorial gain.

options.text: Only minor, unnamed border villages were affected.
options.note: The text names substantial, significant regions, including all of Naphtali, not merely minor villages.

options.text: No territorial changes occurred during Pekah's reign.
options.note: Verse 29 explicitly records major territorial losses during this specific reign.

quiz.q: How is Jotham of Judah's reign evaluated, and what specific building project is noted?

options.text: He did what was right in the LORD's eyes as his father Uzziah had done, though the high places remained, and he rebuilt the Upper Gate of the house of the LORD.
options.note: Verses 34 to 35 give this exact evaluation and the specific building project.

options.text: He was condemned as thoroughly evil like Israel's kings.
options.note: The text explicitly commends him as doing what was right, similar to his father, not condemning him as evil.

options.text: He removed all the high places from Judah completely.
options.note: The text explicitly states the high places were not taken away during his reign.

options.text: No building projects are mentioned during his reign.
options.note: The rebuilding of the Upper Gate of the temple is specifically mentioned in verse 35.

questions: Azariah's discipline came even though his overall reign was described as right in the LORD's eyes. Where might an area of ongoing partial obedience in your own life be inviting a correction you have not yet recognized?

Israel's kings repeatedly seized power through violence rather than legitimate succession, producing constant instability. Where in your own life or community do you see a pattern of striving for control that produces chaos rather than lasting peace?

Menahem paid off a threat rather than trusting the LORD, buying temporary relief at a heavy ongoing cost. Where might you be settling for a costly short-term fix rather than a deeper, more trustworthy solution to a problem you are facing?

Judah's kings, though imperfect, provided far more stability than Israel's constantly overthrown throne. What does consistent, if imperfect, faithfulness over time look like in your own life compared to more dramatic but unstable spiritual highs and lows?

This chapter shows a nation's political chaos as a visible outworking of long ignored spiritual warnings. What repeated warning in your own life might be worth taking more seriously now, before its consequences become as visible as Israel's did here?

translationNotes.note: 2 Chronicles 26:16-21 supplies the specific cause behind Azariah's leprosy, his prideful intrusion into priestly duties in the temple, information this briefer parallel account in Kings does not repeat.

Pul is identified elsewhere as another name for Tiglath-pileser III of Assyria, whose own annals independently record receiving tribute from a king of Israel named Menahem, corroborating this biblical account.

The territories captured by Tiglath-pileser, Galilee, Gilead, and Naphtali, represent Israel's northern and eastern regions, marking the beginning of the systematic Assyrian conquest that will culminate in Samaria's fall in chapter 17.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 2 Kings 15:1-38. No em dashes or en dashes appear in the source text for this chapter.
